title: Nanoplastic pollution? Nanobots to the rescue!
html_body:
Today in Science and science: A bold idea for sparing sea ice, splitting genomes across nuclei, and more
Brought to you by Science Custom Publishing
View this email in your browser
20 April 2026
Today’s Future News reveals a very uncommon behavior discovered in a common mold. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including a new, dark sky map and nanobots that capture nanoplastics.
Geoengineering  |   News from Science
Thick doesn’t stick
Global warming is bad news for polar bears, seals, and walruses; the Arctic sheets of seasonal sea ice they frequent have been on the decline for decades. An ambitious idea seeks to intervene: sea ice thickening, where engineers drill through the ice and pump up ocean water to freeze on top in an attempt to delay summertime melting. If successful, the extra ice would also make the poles brighter, reflecting more of the sun’s radiation back to space and lessening a dangerous climate feedback loop.

Like all geoengineering ideas, it’s controversial. But field trials have begun, and the first results are out: while pumping thickens ice, those gains don’t stick around.

In 2024, two collaborations between researchers and startups took place in Svalbard and the Canadian Arctic, respectively. The former flooded 1500 square meters, and the latter flooded 250,000. While both saw extra ice depth of around 30 centimeters—equivalent to decades of Arctic sea ice loss—it didn’t last any longer than the existing ice when summer temperatures arrived. But the Canadian trial did measure increased ice brightness of about 40%, which they attribute to starting pumping earlier in the winter.

Not all researchers think the results are promising—or scalable. “I don’t know how many experiments we need to do to say [that] even if we did this successfully over the whole Arctic, it still would not be as beneficial as reducing our emissions,” glaciologist Leigh Stearns told ScienceAdviser.
Read the full story
Robotics  |  News from Science
Nanoplastic pollution? Nanobots to the rescue!
These tiny bots may someday ensure astronauts have clean drinking water.  Ramakrishnan and Pumera/Environmental Science: Nano (2026)
The better humans have gotten at making plastic, the more challenging it has been to unmake it—especially the pieces too small to see. Long after plastic bottles and bags break down, they keep fragmenting into particles so tiny, conventional filtration can’t touch them.

Enter nanobots. These tiny magnetic machines made of cagelike materials can tumble through water, sweeping up nanoplastics via the same electrostatic attraction that makes a balloon cling to your hair.

Unlike earlier microrobot designs powered by chemical fuels or ultraviolet light to propel themselves, the bots operate under a low-energy magnetic field and require no additives, making them easier to control and retrieve. When added to a small vial of distilled water spiked with nanoplastics, the bots began to run into the pollutants, snagging them via electrostatic force. After an hour, the moving robots captured 78% of the particles, roughly 60% more than if the bots were sitting completely still.

Real-world water is messier than a lab flask, of course. But the team already has bigger ambitions: Using the bots to clean water filters on the International Space Station.
Read the Full Story
Astronomy  |  Science
Filtering out the Sun’s soft x-ray glare
Isolating radiation from a single source can be challenging in observational astronomy. Lower-energy x-ray surveys, known as soft x-ray surveys, are meant to probe hot gas in and around the galaxy, but observations can also pick up soft x-rays from other sources. Charged particles in the solar wind can steal electrons from gas surrounding the Sun and emit their own soft x-rays, creating a foreground that obscures the galaxy and complicates measurements of the true cosmic x-ray background.

In a new study published in Science, researchers used data from the eROSITA x-ray telescope, located about 1.5 million kilometers from Earth, to try and eliminate some of this foreground and recover a clearer view of the x-ray sky . The telescope repeatedly scanned the same regions of the sky over hours to months, allowing the team to separate stable background emission from a foreground that varies across time and position. Scientists then constructed a “dark sky map” that is largely free of Solar System contamination, estimating that more than 94% of the signal in this map comes from beyond the Solar System, even in regions where the foreground is strongest.

Identifying this foreground is also useful beyond removing noise. By tracking how it changes across time and viewing angles, the team traced where the emission originates. The brightest regions align with structures shaped by the flow of interstellar hydrogen and helium through the Solar System, as they interact with the solar wind. Rather than just getting in the way, this foreground can now be used to better understand the Solar System itself.
Read the paper
SPONSORED
Designing targeted therapies for cancer's next move
Targeted therapies are reshaping cancer care, but tumors evolve to resist them. In this Q&A, Jeff Settleman of Pfizer Oncology discusses strategies to anticipate resistance, target multiple pathways, and design more precise treatments.
Read more
Future News
The mold used to create this East Javan food called oncom partitions its genome into multiple nuclei.  Hariadhi via Wikimedia Commons | CC BY-SA
These fungi don’t keep all their DNA in one place
Some 2.5 to 1.5 billion years ago, some single-celled organisms evolved a new trait: They started packaging their genomes in little pouches called nuclei. Now, the cells of all sorts of living things, from unicellular algae to humans, keep their genomes in these special organelles. And importantly, they keep at least one full set of chromosomes in each one. Sometimes a cell has more than one nucleus inside it—for example, our skeletal muscle cells are formed by the fusion of multiple cells, leading to long tubes with many nuclei—but each of those nuclei contains an entire genome. Even sperm and egg cells have nuclei containing one complete set of chromosomes; things tend to go awry during development if either gamete has too many chromosomes or too few. This has led to what biologists refer to as the “one nucleus, one full genome” rule.

But some fungi apparently didn’t get the memo. Last year, researchers reported that two related species of white mold—both plant pathogens—split their chromosomes seemingly haphazardly between multiple nuclei: Sclerotinia sclerotiorum separates its 16 chromosomes between two nuclei, while Botrytis cinerea distributes its 18 chromosomes across four or five nuclei. Now, that same team of researchers adds the nonpathogenic red bread mold Neurospora crassa to the list—a species from a completely different branch of the fungal family tree that split from the group containing the white molds some 287 million years ago. That this escaped scientists’ notice for so long is especially surprising because N. crassa is a model organism that has been extensively studied since the mid-20th century and was among the first filamentous fungi to have its genome sequenced.

After discovering the two white molds, the researchers wondered how many other fungi might divide up their chromosomes between nuclei. They searched the literature for species that are known to have two or more nuclei in their spores, especially ones where single-nucleus cells are often not viable. N. crassa immediately jumped to the top of the suspect list.

The team then stained the cells’ chromosomes and carefully counted the number in each nucleus. To be even more sure, they used a strain that has glowing histones—which provide clearer pictures of chromosomes—and again, they came up with odd numbers of chromosomes per nucleus. Finally, they specifically tagged two different putative chromosomes with different fluorescent markers. The two never appeared in the same nucleus, confirming that the genome is split apart.

This should be problematic for sexual reproduction, noted cell biologists Timothy Mitchison and William Sullivan in a Science Perspective last year. Although fungi don’t produce sperm and eggs like we do, they still create reproductive cells called spores that contain a complete set of chromosomes. Sometimes, these spores simply settle and grow (asexual reproduction), and sometimes they combine with other spores (sexual reproduction). Either way, a spore not having a complete set of chromosomes would be an issue, so these fungi must have some way of reassembling their chromosomes during the sporemaking process. The failure to do so could explain why up to 60-90% of N. crassa spores with just one nucleus aren’t viable.

The findings lead to more questions than answers. How do the nuclei coordinate to ensure proper gene expression? How do they ensure a complete set of chromosomes ends up in the next generation of spores? And perhaps most perplexingly, why even do this in the first place? What benefit does splitting the genome provide to make up for the myriad risks of such an arrangement?

The team suspects other multinucleate fungi may also wrap their genomes in smaller chunks. “Studying these organisms,” they write, “could provide important insights into the extensiveness of this phenomenon and advance our understanding of chromosome biology and genome evolution.”
Read the preprint
Et Cetera
Breaking through: muon magnetics and more
The winners of this year’s Breakthrough Prizes include the physicists that measured the muon’s magnetic properties, the researchers who developed bold new gene therapies, the mathematician who figured out how to rein in unmanageable non-linear equations, and a scientist who helped better understand string theory.
Read more at Nature
Not a passing thought
The parasites that cause malaria infect more than 1 million children each year. Research now suggests the impacts of infection may go beyond the potentially fatal disease: Kids that had severe malaria in childhood continue to score lower on cognitive and math tests for a decade or more after the illness is over. “It’s in the whole world’s interest to prevent this,” said one pediatric infectious disease specialist.
JAMA Paper  |  Read more at News from Science
Finally, a leader for the CDC
President Donald Trump has nominated physician and lawyer Erica Schwartz to lead the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), a position that has been vacant for months. “It is encouraging to learn of her nomination and I hope it will signal a better phase for CDC and public health,” said one epidemiologist and former CDC deputy director. “This is a strong candidate for the position.”
Read more at ScienceInsider
Mosquitoes in Iceland are more than a curiosity or future annoyance. They are a warning that the Arctic lacks a system for monitoring arthropods and anticipating biological risks before they escalate.
EDITORIAL  |  16 April 2026  |  Amanda M. Koltz and Lauren E. Culler
Last but not least
When I scuba dive, I don’t worry about the sharks. It’s the seals and sea lions that scare me—and I have a feeling this pufferfish would agree.

(Though, like with the dolphins that enjoy underwater fishball games, I don’t believe they’re intentionally getting high.)
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Hannah Richter, David Grimm, and Ana Georgescu

raw_metadata.subject: Nanoplastic pollution? Nanobots to the rescue!
